text: Chapter 5 establishes the foundational taxonomy and mechanics of chord construction on guitar. The chapter teaches that chord formulas are built relative to the major scale with the same root, and introduces three structural categories: FORMULA (the tones used to build a chord), TRIAD (a three-tone chord), and three chord families—MAJOR, MINOR, and DOMINANT 7TH—from which all other chords derive. Key extensions and modifications include EXTENSIONS (tones added to basic chords), ALTERED chords (containing a #5th, b5th, b9th, or #9th), and SUSPENDED chords (where the 3rd is replaced by a 4th). The master's characteristic moves center on voicing discipline: the same-string inversion method (raising each tone on its string to the next higher tone in the chord) yields a memorable rule that any N-note chord has exactly N different forms on the same set of strings, along with practical guidelines for omitting or doubling tones — altered 5ths must be retained, roots or 5ths may be omitted in five-note chords, and 3rds are often omitted in 11th chords. The chapter also clarifies load-bearing terminology including INVERSIONS or VOICINGS, the compound interval naming convention (9th/11th/13th from piano theory), and voicing rules for altered extensions like placing #9 above the 3rd and +11 above the 5th.
